methodologies.name: Auction-Based Task Allocation
methodologies.summary: Allocate tasks across a population of self-interested agents by running an incentive-compatible auction — typically Vickrey (second-price sealed-bid) or VCG (Vickrey–Clarke–Groves) for combinatorial bundles. Truthful bidding becomes the dominant strategy, so the mechanism elicits each agent's real valuation rather than its strategic misrepresentation, and the allocation maximises social welfare. Distinct from contract-net protocol as a generic structural pattern: this methodology is the mechanism-design procedure for choosing the auction form, defining the payment rule, and proving the strategy-proofness property the deployment depends on.
methodologies.intent: Choose and instantiate an incentive-compatible auction mechanism so that self-interested agents reveal their true valuations and the resulting task allocation is efficient.
methodologies.when_to_apply: Allocating scarce tasks or resources across multiple self-interested agents whose private valuations cannot be assumed to be reported truthfully under a naive allocation rule. Don't apply when agents are fully cooperative — incentive compatibility is overhead you don't need. Exceptions: even cooperative settings sometimes benefit from auction mechanics to break ties or surface preferences cheaply.

inputs.name: Bidder population
inputs.description: The set of agents that will bid, each with a private valuation function over the goods being allocated.

inputs.name: Goods or tasks to allocate
inputs.description: The items being auctioned — single goods, bundles, or task contracts with deliverables.

inputs.name: Social objective
inputs.description: What 'good allocation' means — typically welfare-maximising; sometimes revenue-maximising, sometimes fairness-constrained.

outputs.name: Auction mechanism
outputs.description: Specified auction form (Vickrey, VCG, English, Dutch, first-price sealed-bid) with bid format, winner determination, and payment rule.

outputs.name: Truthfulness proof or argument
outputs.description: Demonstration that truthful bidding is a dominant strategy under the chosen mechanism — or an explicit acknowledgement of where this fails.

outputs.name: Allocation and payments
outputs.description: Per-round assignment of goods to agents and the payment each agent owes.

diagram.mermaid:
sequenceDiagram
  participant A as Auctioneer
  participant B1 as Bidder 1
  participant B2 as Bidder 2
  participant BN as Bidder N
  Note over A: announce goods, mechanism, payment rule
  A->>B1: call for sealed bids
  A->>B2: call for sealed bids
  A->>BN: call for sealed bids
  B1-->>A: bid b1
  B2-->>A: bid b2
  BN-->>A: bid bN
  Note over A: winner determination (sort or VCG optimisation)
  A->>A: compute payment rule (Vickrey: 2nd-price; VCG: externality)
  A-->>B1: allocation + payment
  A-->>B2: allocation + payment
  A-->>BN: allocation + payment
  Note over A: log bids + computation for audit

methodologies.example_scenario:
A cloud-cost team at a media company runs an internal 'spot capacity' market: every hour, idle GPU minutes on the rendering cluster get allocated to the data-science teams whose training jobs are queued. Teams have private valuations for capacity (one team's quarterly model training is worth far more than another's exploratory notebook). A naive 'first-come-first-served' allocation rewarded teams that gamed the queue by submitting placeholder jobs early.

Applying the methodology, the cost team modelled team utilities as quasilinear (utility = valuation − internal chargeback). For single-GPU-hour slots they adopted a Vickrey (second-price sealed-bid) auction; truthful bidding became the dominant strategy and the placeholder-gaming evaporated overnight. For bundled training runs that needed N consecutive GPU-hours, they generalised to VCG with a 30-second cap on the winner-determination optimisation and a documented approximation gap. The payment rule was implemented as a small service. They verified assumptions: bids were sealed (an API not a Slack thread), collusion was bounded by the small number of bidders and an audit log, and budgets were generous enough not to bind below equilibrium.

What they learned: the audit log caught one assumption violation in month two — two teams had agreed informally to alternate weeks of high bidding. Mechanism-design correctness had not prevented the collusion, but the audit revealed it within 48 hours of the first incident. They added a soft policy and made bid distributions visible to leadership. Allocation efficiency, measured by the company-wide chargeback model, improved 17% over the naive baseline.

steps.name: Specify quasilinear preferences and valuations
steps.description: Model each bidder's preference as utility = valuation − payment. Document the valuation domain — single-item, bundle, function over outcomes — because the mechanism depends on this shape.

steps.name: Pick an incentive-compatible auction form
steps.description: For single-item allocation, Vickrey (second-price sealed-bid) makes truthful bidding dominant. For bundles or combinatorial allocations, VCG generalises the property. First-price and English auctions are not strategy-proof — pick them only when budget-balance or simplicity outweighs incentive compatibility.

steps.name: Define the winner-determination algorithm
steps.description: Specify how winners are computed from bids — a sort for single-item, a (NP-hard in the worst case) optimisation for combinatorial. Acknowledge the computational cost; bound it with approximation if needed.

steps.name: Specify the payment rule
steps.description: For Vickrey, winners pay the second-highest bid. For VCG, each winner pays the externality they impose on the rest. Get this rule wrong and incentive compatibility evaporates.

steps.name: Verify implementation theory assumptions
steps.description: Check that the deployment satisfies the assumptions the theorem rests on — sealed bids, no collusion, no budget constraints below the equilibrium, single dimensional preferences for Vickrey. Where assumptions fail, document the residual strategic risk.

steps.name: Instrument and audit
steps.description: Log every bid, every winner-determination computation, and every payment. Mechanism-design correctness is verifiable only after the fact; without logs you cannot detect manipulation or implementation drift.

principles: Incentive compatibility is a contract: pick the mechanism that makes truthful bidding dominant, then prove it for your setting.
The payment rule is not optional decoration — it is what enforces truthfulness.
Winner determination is computationally bounded; combinatorial cases need approximations with documented optimality gaps.
Strategy-proofness rests on assumptions (sealed bids, no collusion) — name where the deployment breaks them.

known_failure_modes.note: Bidders discover an assumption violation (collusion, side-payments, repeated-game dynamics) that the mechanism's truthfulness proof did not cover.
Combinatorial winner-determination in VCG without a computational cap — the allocation step never terminates on hard instances.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition, ed. Gerhard Weiss, MIT Press 2013)
sources.chapter: Ch 7 'Mechanism Design and Auctions' — Kevin Leyton-Brown and Yoav Shoham — §1 Introduction; §2 Mechanism Design with Unrestricted Preferences; §3 Quasilinear Preferences; §4 Efficient Mechanisms; §5 Single-Good Auctions; §6 Position Auctions; §7 Combinatorial Auctions; §8 Conclusions
sources.quote: Mechanism Design and Auctions — Kevin Leyton-Brown and Yoav Shoham — 1 Introduction, 2 Mechanism Design with Unrestricted Preferences, 3 Quasilinear Preferences, 4 Efficient Mechanisms, 5 Single-Good Auctions, 6 Position Auctions, 7 Combinatorial Auctions, 8 Conclusions

sources.title: Counterspeculation, Auctions, and Competitive Sealed Tenders (William Vickrey, 1961)
sources.quote: Vickrey, W. (1961). Counterspeculation, Auctions, and Competitive Sealed Tenders. The Journal of Finance, 16(1), 8-37. DOI: 10.1111/j.1540-6261.1961.tb02789.x — the foundational second-price-sealed-bid auction paper underlying VCG mechanism design.

methodologies.name: Voting-Based Group Decision
methodologies.summary: Aggregate heterogeneous agent preferences into a single group decision by choosing a voting rule whose axiomatic properties match the deployment's needs — Condorcet consistency, monotonicity, anti-manipulability, fair division. The methodology is the social-choice procedure for choosing the rule, eliciting preferences in the right format, and acknowledging the impossibility results (Arrow, Gibbard–Satterthwaite) that no rule escapes. Distinct from majority-vote-as-default: the rule choice is itself the design decision, and different rules can disagree on the same ballots.
methodologies.intent: Pick a voting rule whose axiomatic properties match the group-decision requirements, then aggregate agent preferences under that rule with documented awareness of its limitations.
methodologies.when_to_apply: When multiple agents hold heterogeneous preferences over a finite set of alternatives and a single group choice must be made — task ranking, alternative selection, configuration vote, multi-agent debate resolution. Don't apply when one agent has authoritative preference (just delegate to it), or when preferences are not articulable (cardinal preferences may be more honest than forced ranking). Exceptions: even in single-agent settings, voting rules can aggregate multiple internal critics or model votes.

inputs.name: Agent preferences
inputs.description: Each agent's preference over the alternatives — ranking, scoring, approval set, or pairwise comparison, depending on the elicitation.

inputs.name: Alternative set
inputs.description: The finite set of options being voted on — candidates, plans, tasks, configurations.

inputs.name: Desired axiomatic properties
inputs.description: Which properties matter — Condorcet, monotonicity, anonymity, neutrality, Pareto, manipulability — ranked by what the deployment cannot live without.

outputs.name: Selected voting rule
outputs.description: The chosen rule — plurality, Borda, approval, Condorcet method, STV, Copeland, etc. — with the rationale grounded in the desired properties.

outputs.name: Group decision
outputs.description: The single alternative (or ranking) the rule produces from the agent preferences.

outputs.name: Impossibility caveat
outputs.description: An explicit note of which axiomatic properties the chosen rule violates — Arrow guarantees you can't have them all.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  prefs[Agent preferences + alternative set] --> rank[Rank desired axiomatic properties]
  rank --> elicit[Elicit in format the rule consumes]
  elicit --> pick{Pick voting rule}
  pick -- top-only --> plur[Plurality]
  pick -- full ranking --> borda[Borda count]
  pick -- pairwise winner --> cond[Condorcet method e.g. Copeland or Schulze]
  pick -- approval set --> appr[Approval voting]
  pick -- proportional --> stv[STV]
  plur --> tie[Specify tie-break]
  borda --> tie
  cond --> tie
  appr --> tie
  stv --> tie
  tie --> agg[Run aggregation on ballots]
  agg --> caveat[Document Gibbard-Satterthwaite manipulation surface]
  caveat --> audit[Audit ballots + outcome]
  audit --> out[Group decision + impossibility caveat]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A platform group at a fintech runs a quarterly 'engineering priorities' vote across seven feature teams. The default had been plurality — each team picks one favourite from the candidate roadmap items — and the winner was usually a fragmenting choice that nobody hated but few teams strongly wanted. After two quarters of dissatisfaction, the platform lead ran the voting-based group-decision methodology.

They ranked the properties they cared about: Pareto efficiency (don't pick something a unanimously better option exists for), Condorcet consistency (if a pairwise winner exists, pick it), and resistance to obvious manipulation. Plurality failed Condorcet badly on their historical ballots. Borda count was Condorcet-failing in one out of three replays, and the legal team flagged it as manipulable via clone candidates. They picked the Schulze method (a Condorcet rule with a documented tie-break by path-strength). Elicitation: each team submitted a full ranking of the 12 candidate items via a small internal app. Tie-break and manipulability caveats were documented in the announcement.

What they learned: re-running historical ballots showed Schulze would have elected a different winner in two of the prior three quarters — both winners that, post hoc, the teams preferred. The first live Schulze election produced a clear Condorcet winner. They documented the Gibbard-Satterthwaite exposure honestly (every non-dictatorial rule is manipulable somewhere) and identified two specific manipulation patterns to watch for in audits. Different rules really do disagree on the same ballots — making the rule choice explicit moved the conversation from 'who won' to 'are we choosing well.'

steps.name: Elicit preferences in the right format
steps.description: Rankings, scores, approval sets, and pairwise comparisons are not interchangeable. Pick the elicitation format the chosen voting rule consumes — and pick it before you pick the rule, if elicitation cost dominates.

steps.name: Rank the axiomatic properties you need
steps.description: Condorcet consistency, monotonicity, manipulability resistance, anonymity, Pareto efficiency. Arrow's impossibility theorem says no rule satisfies all desirable properties for ≥3 alternatives — so rank yours and accept the losses you can tolerate.

steps.name: Pick a voting rule whose property profile matches
steps.description: Plurality is simple but ignores everything below the top choice. Borda count uses full rankings but is manipulable. Condorcet methods (Copeland, Schulze) elect the pairwise winner when one exists. Approval voting reduces strategic complexity. STV gives proportionality.

steps.name: Run the aggregation
steps.description: Apply the rule to the elicited ballots. If the rule has a cyclic-preference edge case (Condorcet paradox), specify the tie-break — and treat the tie-break as part of the rule, not as an afterthought.

steps.name: Document the manipulability surface
steps.description: Gibbard–Satterthwaite: every non-dictatorial voting rule over ≥3 alternatives is manipulable in some preference profile. Identify which manipulations the deployment is exposed to; if the agents are strategic, this is a live risk.

steps.name: Audit ballots and outcomes
steps.description: Log every ballot and every rule application. Re-run the rule offline to confirm. Different rules can elect different winners on the same ballots; an audit makes the rule choice visible after the fact.

principles: The voting rule is the design decision; majority-vote-as-default is choosing without choosing.
Arrow's impossibility says you can't have every desirable property — rank them, accept the losses, document.
Elicitation format and rule must match — rankings for Borda, approvals for approval voting; conversion loses information.
Every non-dictatorial rule is manipulable somewhere (Gibbard–Satterthwaite); name where yours is.

known_failure_modes.note: Condorcet cycles trigger endless re-voting because no tie-break was specified up front.
When agents share a model family their preferences cluster — the 'vote' becomes a single agent in many disguises.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition, ed. Gerhard Weiss, MIT Press 2013)
sources.chapter: Ch 6 'Computational Social Choice' — Felix Brandt, Vincent Conitzer, and Ulle Endriss — §1 Introduction; §2 Preference Aggregation; §3 Voting; §4 Combinatorial Domains; §5 Fair Division; §6 Conclusion
sources.quote: Computational Social Choice — Felix Brandt, Vincent Conitzer, and Ulle Endriss — 1 Introduction, 2 Preference Aggregation, 3 Voting, 4 Combinatorial Domains, 5 Fair Division, 6 Conclusion

sources.title: Social Choice and Individual Values (Kenneth J. Arrow, Yale University Press, 1951; rev. 1963)
sources.chapter: Ch V 'The General Possibility Theorem for Social Welfare Functions' — proves Arrow's Impossibility Theorem
sources.quote: Arrow, K. J. (1951). Social Choice and Individual Values. New York: Wiley. — establishes Arrow's General Possibility (Impossibility) Theorem: absent restrictions on individual preferences, no social welfare function jointly satisfies Unrestricted Domain, Pareto efficiency, Independence of Irrelevant Alternatives, and Non-Dictatorship.

methodologies.name: Coalition Formation
methodologies.summary: Form value-maximising coalitions of agents by representing the characteristic function over coalitions, generating a coalition structure that maximises total value, and dividing the payoff using a solution concept (Shapley value, core, nucleolus) that captures the fairness or stability property the deployment needs. Distinct from ad-hoc team formation: this methodology gives explicit representations for the characteristic function, an account of which solution concepts are computable in practice, and the trade-offs between fairness and stability the chosen concept embodies.
methodologies.intent: Form coalitions among agents that maximise joint value and divide payoffs by a solution concept whose stability or fairness property matches the deployment.
methodologies.when_to_apply: When multiple agents could combine resources or capabilities for super-additive value — joint planning, resource pooling, federated computation, multi-agent task delivery — and the question of who joins which coalition (and who gets what share of the payoff) is itself the coordination problem. Don't apply when agents are interchangeable and any partition is fine — simpler partitioning suffices. Exceptions: even interchangeable agents may benefit from coalition formation if coalition-internal coordination cost is non-trivial.

inputs.name: Agent population
inputs.description: The set of agents that may form coalitions, with their individual capabilities and interaction costs.

inputs.name: Characteristic function
inputs.description: A function v(S) giving the value each coalition S can achieve, represented compactly (synergy coalitions, marginal-contribution nets, etc.) when |agents| is large.

inputs.name: Solution concept
inputs.description: The chosen payoff-division rule — Shapley value (fairness-oriented), core (stability-oriented), nucleolus (minimise max dissatisfaction), kernel.

outputs.name: Coalition structure
outputs.description: A partition of the agent population into coalitions that (approximately) maximises total value Σ v(S).

outputs.name: Payoff division
outputs.description: How the value of each coalition is divided among its members under the chosen solution concept.

outputs.name: Stability or fairness certificate
outputs.description: Evidence that the chosen division satisfies its concept — core non-emptiness, Shapley axioms, nucleolus optimality — or an explicit note where it does not.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  agents[Agent population + capabilities] --> rep[Choose compact representation of v(S)]
  rep --> gen[Generate coalition structure: maximise sum v(S)]
  gen --> sc{Pick solution concept}
  sc -- fairness --> sh[Shapley value: average marginal contribution]
  sc -- stability --> core{Core non-empty?}
  sc -- always-exists --> nuc[Nucleolus: minimise max dissatisfaction]
  core -- yes --> coreok[Core payoff]
  core -- no --> eps[Fall back to least-core / epsilon-core / nucleolus]
  sh --> div[Compute payoff division]
  coreok --> div
  eps --> div
  nuc --> div
  div --> verify[Verify stability + fairness properties]
  verify --> out[Coalition structure + payoff + certificate]
  out -.value landscape shifts.-> agents

methodologies.example_scenario:
An edge-computing startup operates a federated inference network: independent operators contribute GPU nodes and split the revenue from serving inference jobs. The question of which operators form which serving pools — and how revenue from a pool is divided — is not trivial. Big operators have raw capacity; smaller operators may have rare hardware (Hopper cards) or favourable locations (low-latency to a major customer).

Applying the methodology, the platform team modelled each subset S of operators as a coalition with characteristic value v(S) = expected revenue if those operators served jointly. With 14 operators, the full 2^14 enumeration was feasible to compute once a week from logs, but they used a marginal-contribution-network representation that captured the synergies (e.g. 'Hopper card + EU-West location' bonus) more compactly. Coalition structure generation was an anytime branch-and-bound capped at 30 seconds, with the best partition found in that budget kept. For payoff division they chose Shapley value because operators cared more about being seen as fairly compensated than about strict stability — the platform's reputation depended on it. They computed Shapley by Monte Carlo sampling over orderings.

What they learned: re-formation every Monday caught a slow drift — an operator who had been pivotal lost a key customer's traffic and stopped contributing marginal value; the new Shapley division reflected that within a week, avoiding a quarter-long dispute. They documented two known violations honestly: the Shapley division was sometimes not in the core (a sub-coalition could in principle do better by defecting), and they accepted that trade-off because fairness optics outweighed strict stability. The hero-agent failure mode showed up once — a single operator was contributing 60% of value — and they used that signal to recruit redundant capacity.

steps.name: Choose a representation for the characteristic function
steps.description: Listing v(S) for every subset is 2^n entries and infeasible past small n. Use a compact representation — synergy coalition groups, marginal-contribution networks, weighted graphs — that captures the structure of the value function with manageable size.

steps.name: Generate the coalition structure
steps.description: Find the partition of agents that maximises Σ v(S). This is in general NP-hard; use exact algorithms for small populations, anytime branch-and-bound for medium populations, heuristics with bounds for large ones.

steps.name: Pick a solution concept
steps.description: Shapley value: each agent paid their average marginal contribution — fair but not always stable. Core: no sub-coalition can break off and do better — stable but may be empty. Nucleolus: minimises maximum dissatisfaction — always exists, may be expensive to compute.

steps.name: Compute the payoff division
steps.description: Apply the chosen solution concept. Document the computational cost. For the core, check non-emptiness first; if the core is empty, fall back to a relaxed concept (least-core, ε-core) or switch to nucleolus.

steps.name: Verify stability and fairness properties
steps.description: Stability check: for each agent, would any sub-coalition give it a strictly better payoff? Fairness check: does the division satisfy symmetry, dummy, and additivity? If violations are tolerated, document them — silent violation destroys trust in the mechanism.

steps.name: Re-plan when the agent population or values shift
steps.description: Characteristic functions are not stationary — new agents arrive, capabilities change, prices move. Schedule re-formation rather than treating the first coalition as permanent.

principles: Characteristic function representation must be compact — listing all 2^n coalition values is rarely feasible.
Solution concept choice is fairness-vs-stability trade-off; pick deliberately and document the trade.
Coalition structure generation is NP-hard in general — bound it explicitly with anytime algorithms.
Coalitions are not permanent; schedule re-formation when the value landscape shifts.

known_failure_modes.note: Coalition structure generation runs forever on hard instances when no anytime cap or approximation bound is specified.
Solution concept is computed once, then ignored as the value landscape shifts — coalitions become stale and unfair.
One agent contributes the bulk of every coalition's value; payoff division reflects that, but the system silently bottlenecks on that single agent.

sources.title: Multiagent Systems (2nd edition, ed. Gerhard Weiss, MIT Press 2013)
sources.chapter: Ch 8 'Computational Coalition Formation' — Edith Elkind, Talal Rahwan, and Nicholas R. Jennings — §1 Introduction; §2 Definitions; §3 Solution Concepts; §4 Representation Formalisms; §5 Coalition Structure Generation; §6 Conclusions
sources.quote: Computational Coalition Formation — Edith Elkind, Talal Rahwan, and Nicholas R. Jennings — 1 Introduction, 2 Definitions, 3 Solution Concepts, 4 Representation Formalisms, 5 Coalition Structure Generation, 6 Conclusions

sources.title: A Value for n-Person Games (Lloyd S. Shapley, 1953)
sources.quote: Shapley, L. S. (1953). A Value for n-Person Games. In H. W. Kuhn & A. W. Tucker (Eds.), Contributions to the Theory of Games, Volume II (Annals of Mathematics Studies, 28), pp. 307-317. Princeton University Press. — defines the unique cooperative-game payoff division satisfying symmetry, dummy, additivity, and efficiency axioms.
